content.introduction: Merge sort divides arrays recursively and merges sorted halves, providing stable O(n log n) performance.

sections.title: 7.6.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define divide-and-conquer structure and sorted-merge invariant.
examples: Explain why merge sort is stable
Contrast merge strategy with quick partition

sections.title: 7.6.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe recursive splitting, merge procedure, and auxiliary buffer usage.
examples: Merge two sorted lists step by step
Handle duplicate values while preserving order

sections.title: 7.6.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: Complexity is O(n log n) time and O(n) extra space; discuss stability and external-sort friendliness.
examples: Use merge sort for linked lists and stable sorting
Compare memory overhead with in-place sorts
Apply merge strategy in external sorting pipelines

keyTakeaways: Guarantee O(n log n) sorting time
Use merge sort where stability is required
